title: Software Engineering Competency Matrix
description: A multidimensional matrix linking competencies to technologies, frameworks, languages, and adjacent disciplines including data science, machine learning, and AI engineering across career levels.

design_principles: Competencies are primary. Technologies are evidence, not competencies by themselves.
Coverage is intentionally broad across mainstream modern engineering stacks.
This is designed for assessment, hiring, promotion, and capability mapping.

mid.scope: Owns features or bounded components end to end

senior.scope: Owns systems, quality, and technical decisions across a team area

staff.scope: Shapes architecture and technical direction across multiple teams

principal.scope: Defines engineering strategy, standards, and system direction across the organization

levels.junior: Writes readable code, understands control flow, functions, modules, types, and basic debugging.
levels.mid: Uses language idioms appropriately, structures modules well, and handles common edge cases independently.
levels.senior: Uses advanced language features judiciously, reduces complexity, and raises code quality across the team.
levels.staff: Defines language usage patterns, shared abstractions, and coding standards across teams.
levels.principal: Makes strategic language and platform choices based on long-term maintainability, talent availability, and business leverage.

levels.junior: Understands basic collections, recursion, iteration, and simple complexity analysis.
levels.mid: Chooses appropriate structures and avoids obvious performance pitfalls.
levels.senior: Balances readability, latency, throughput, and memory tradeoffs in production systems.
levels.staff: Defines where algorithmic optimization matters and how teams should evaluate performance-critical code.
levels.principal: Sets organizational guidance on where complexity investment is justified and where simpler solutions should win.

levels.junior: Follows naming, structure, formatting, and review conventions.
levels.mid: Refactors safely, improves cohesion, and avoids accidental complexity.
levels.senior: Systematically improves maintainability and mentors others on code health.
levels.staff: Defines maintainability standards, shared libraries, and code review expectations across domains.
levels.principal: Drives simplification strategy and long-term reduction of technical entropy across the organization.

levels.junior: Uses branches, commits, pull requests, and basic review workflows correctly.
levels.mid: Structures changes well, resolves conflicts, and reviews straightforward work effectively.
levels.senior: Uses review to improve design quality and delivery safety, not only correctness.
levels.staff: Defines repository, branching, and review strategies across teams.
levels.principal: Shapes company-wide software development workflow for velocity, traceability, and governance.

levels.junior: Writes basic unit tests and covers happy paths plus simple edge cases.
levels.mid: Writes maintainable unit, integration, and API tests and understands when to use mocks versus real dependencies.
levels.senior: Builds effective test strategies, reduces flakiness, and improves confidence in delivery.
levels.staff: Defines quality gates, test pyramid expectations, and reusable testing patterns across systems.
levels.principal: Sets organizational confidence strategy balancing reliability, delivery speed, and operating cost.

levels.junior: Consumes APIs correctly and implements basic endpoints or integrations.
levels.mid: Designs predictable APIs with validation, pagination, error handling, and authentication.
levels.senior: Designs versioning, idempotency, backward compatibility, and sound service boundaries.
levels.staff: Defines cross-team API standards, contract governance, and integration patterns.
levels.principal: Sets enterprise integration strategy across synchronous, asynchronous, internal, and external interfaces.

levels.junior: Writes simple queries and understands CRUD, indexes, and transactional basics.
levels.mid: Designs schemas for a bounded service, adds indexes appropriately, and handles consistency concerns.
levels.senior: Models data for performance, auditability, scale, and lifecycle management.
levels.staff: Defines patterns for polyglot persistence, ownership boundaries, and shared data concerns.
levels.principal: Sets enterprise data architecture strategy across operational, analytical, and regulatory constraints.

levels.junior: Understands asynchronous workflows, eventual consistency, and common failure modes conceptually.
levels.mid: Implements retries, idempotency, timeouts, dead-letter handling, and safe message processing.
levels.senior: Designs resilient service interactions, boundaries, and recovery behavior.
levels.staff: Shapes reliability and integration patterns across teams and shared platform capabilities.
levels.principal: Defines distributed systems principles and tradeoff frameworks for the company.

levels.junior: Understands the architecture they work within and respects existing boundaries.
levels.mid: Designs features within an established architecture and reasons about component boundaries.
levels.senior: Designs services, applications, or subsystems with explicit tradeoffs and operational implications.
levels.staff: Leads architecture across multiple teams and resolves cross-cutting design conflicts.
levels.principal: Defines the target architecture and evolution strategy across the organization.

levels.junior: Follows secure coding basics and handles secrets and credentials correctly.
levels.mid: Implements authentication, authorization, validation, dependency hygiene, and secure defaults.
levels.senior: Designs threat-aware systems with layered controls and operational safeguards.
levels.staff: Defines security guardrails, reusable secure patterns, and platform-level controls.
levels.principal: Aligns security architecture with regulatory posture, business risk, and strategic growth.

levels.junior: Recognizes common reliability failure modes and basic mitigation concepts.
levels.mid: Implements safe retries, backoff, circuit breaking, and operationally sound failure handling.
levels.senior: Designs for fault isolation, graceful degradation, recovery, and predictable failure domains.
levels.staff: Defines SLO-oriented reliability patterns and platform standards across teams.
levels.principal: Makes resilience an organizational design constraint with explicit investment and cost tradeoffs.

levels.junior: Adds useful logs, metrics, and traces with guidance.
levels.mid: Instruments services and jobs meaningfully and uses dashboards and traces during debugging.
levels.senior: Designs observability for diagnosis, incident response, capacity planning, and service learning.
levels.staff: Defines telemetry schemas, alert quality standards, and shared observability practices.
levels.principal: Shapes organization-wide visibility strategy for systems, models, and AI applications.

levels.junior: Understands basic latency, throughput, memory, and startup cost concepts.
levels.mid: Measures bottlenecks and fixes obvious inefficiencies in code, queries, or pipelines.
levels.senior: Designs applications, data pipelines, and inference paths for predictable performance under load.
levels.staff: Defines budgets, benchmarking strategy, and capacity tradeoffs across domains.
levels.principal: Determines where performance is strategically differentiating and funds the right optimization work.

levels.junior: Uses pipelines, local tooling, and build systems effectively and resolves basic issues.
levels.mid: Contributes automation, improves build reliability, and removes repetitive development friction.
levels.senior: Designs delivery pipelines with quality gates, rollback paths, and reproducible workflows.
levels.staff: Defines platform delivery standards, reusable workflows, and shared developer tooling.
levels.principal: Sets company-level delivery model for speed, governance, auditability, and developer leverage.

levels.junior: Understands environments, containers, networking basics, and managed services at a practical level.
levels.mid: Deploys, debugs, and operates applications in cloud environments safely.
levels.senior: Designs runtime, storage, network, tenancy, and cost tradeoffs deliberately.
levels.staff: Defines infrastructure patterns, platform abstractions, and tenancy or region strategies.
levels.principal: Sets long-term infrastructure, cloud, sovereignty, and platform investment strategy.

levels.junior: Builds basic components, forms, and client-side state with guidance.
levels.mid: Delivers complete features including routing, API integration, accessibility basics, and error handling.
levels.senior: Owns frontend architecture quality, performance, design system usage, and maintainability.
levels.staff: Defines frontend conventions, platform standards, and reusable UI foundations.
levels.principal: Aligns frontend architecture with product strategy and multi-surface consistency.

levels.junior: Ships scoped screens and handles platform basics.
levels.mid: Owns features involving state, networking, lifecycle, device integration, and release readiness.
levels.senior: Designs maintainable mobile architecture, telemetry, offline behavior, and performance.
levels.staff: Sets mobile platform standards and shared foundations across apps or teams.
levels.principal: Defines organization-wide mobile strategy across native, cross-platform, and distribution constraints.

levels.junior: Writes SQL, basic transformations, and simple batch or streaming jobs.
levels.mid: Builds dependable pipelines, data models, and validations with clear ownership.
levels.senior: Designs lineage-aware, SLA-driven data systems for analytical and operational use.
levels.staff: Defines data contracts, storage patterns, and cross-team platform standards.
levels.principal: Sets the enterprise data platform direction across warehouse, lakehouse, streaming, and governance needs.

levels.junior: Performs exploratory analysis, feature preparation, and basic statistical reasoning.
levels.mid: Builds reproducible analysis, selects metrics, and evaluates models with sound statistical discipline.
levels.senior: Designs experiments, interprets uncertainty, and guides reliable analytical decision-making.
levels.staff: Defines scientific standards, reproducibility expectations, and model evaluation frameworks.
levels.principal: Aligns data science direction with product, risk, and organizational decision quality.

levels.junior: Builds simple training pipelines and understands features, labels, metrics, and train-test separation.
levels.mid: Implements reproducible training, validation, tuning, and packaging for deployment.
levels.senior: Designs production-grade model systems including data interfaces, serving paths, monitoring, and rollback.
levels.staff: Defines model architecture patterns, serving standards, and ML platform expectations across teams.
levels.principal: Sets company-wide ML engineering strategy spanning experimentation, deployment, governance, and value capture.

levels.junior: Builds basic prompt-driven features and understands core concepts like embeddings, retrieval, and tool calling.
levels.mid: Implements RAG, structured outputs, evaluation loops, safety checks, and provider integration responsibly.
levels.senior: Designs reliable AI applications with retrieval quality, latency control, fallback behavior, observability, and risk mitigation.
levels.staff: Defines AI architecture patterns, evaluation standards, platform abstractions, and guardrail strategies across teams.
levels.principal: Sets enterprise AI application strategy across model selection, governance, risk, economics, and long-term capability building.

levels.junior: Understands experiment tracking, versioning, and the basics of model deployment and monitoring.
levels.mid: Operates training and deployment pipelines with reproducibility, traceability, and rollback support.
levels.senior: Designs model lifecycle systems covering feature management, registry, deployment, drift, and alerting.
levels.staff: Defines shared MLOps platform standards and integrations across data, ML, and application teams.
levels.principal: Sets organization-wide model operations strategy across compliance, scale, cost, and deployment models.

levels.junior: Understands the business workflow for assigned features.
levels.mid: Maps requirements into entities, workflows, and service boundaries coherently.
levels.senior: Shapes domain boundaries and models invariants explicitly across subsystems.
levels.staff: Resolves domain ambiguity across products, teams, and integration boundaries.
levels.principal: Defines business capability maps and system boundaries across the organization.

levels.junior: Understands the user problem behind the assigned task.
levels.mid: Makes sensible tradeoffs between scope, quality, speed, and user value.
levels.senior: Influences roadmap through technical insight, metrics awareness, and user impact reasoning.
levels.staff: Connects technical direction to product outcomes across teams and surfaces.
levels.principal: Guides company-level product and engineering tradeoffs and strategic bets.

levels.junior: Explains implementation decisions clearly in code, comments, and pull requests.
levels.mid: Writes usable docs and communicates tradeoffs effectively in team contexts.
levels.senior: Produces strong design docs, review feedback, and incident analysis.
levels.staff: Creates alignment across teams through crisp technical narratives and structured decision-making.
levels.principal: Influences engineering leaders and executives through clear technical strategy communication.

levels.junior: Delivers scoped tasks reliably and surfaces blockers early.
levels.mid: Breaks down features, manages dependencies, and executes with reasonable predictability.
levels.senior: De-risks delivery, sequences work well, and handles cross-cutting concerns proactively.
levels.staff: Coordinates technical execution across teams, streams, or programs.
levels.principal: Converts strategic goals into realistic engineering execution models with explicit tradeoffs.

levels.junior: Accepts feedback well and improves through coaching.
levels.mid: Gives useful peer feedback and helps teammates informally.
levels.senior: Mentors engineers actively and raises the technical bar for the team.
levels.staff: Develops senior engineers and leads communities of practice.
levels.principal: Builds technical leadership bench strength and engineering culture at scale.

levels.junior: Participates effectively in incident handling with support.
levels.mid: Triages, debugs, and resolves routine incidents and follow-up tasks.
levels.senior: Leads major incident response and drives durable prevention work.
levels.staff: Defines readiness, severity, escalation, and learning-loop standards across teams.
levels.principal: Sets the organizational operating model for resilience, incident learning, and risk response.

levels.junior: Follows required controls, process, and documentation expectations.
levels.mid: Understands why controls exist and implements the needed technical evidence or process hooks.
levels.senior: Builds compliant systems with minimal delivery friction and clear auditability.
levels.staff: Designs compliant-by-default platforms, policies, and reusable controls.
levels.principal: Aligns engineering governance with regulation, enterprise trust, data usage rules, and strategic risk posture.
